content.text: For GraphQL + Claude backends, the best schema design is to model your API around product-level conversation workflows—sessions, messages, runs, tools, usage, and streaming events—rather than mirroring the raw Claude Messages API 1:1. GraphQL schema design guidance strongly recommends schemas that reflect client use cases, use clear object/input types, global IDs, pagination, and explicit nullability, while Claude’s Messages API remains stateless and expects the application to manage conversation history itself.

A conversational AI app built on Claude usually has six domain concepts:
-

: a durable chat/session object your app owns.
-

: a user, assistant, system, or tool-related turn.
-

: a single Claude invocation against a conversation state.
-

: structured tool requests/results that may occur inside a run.
-

: token/cost/latency metadata for billing and observability.
-
: partial deltas and lifecycle events for live UI updates.

The schema should expose those concepts directly, because GraphQL design guidance says the schema should match frontend needs and user workflows, not backend tables or provider transport details. In plain language: your clients want to ask for “conversation, its messages, latest run status, and usage,” not “give me Anthropic request JSON.”
A strong GraphQL schema for Claude apps follows these principles:
Model product concepts, not provider payloads
Use globally unique IDs and a Node-style pattern where helpful
Use unions/interfaces for polymorphic content and events
so clients know what is guaranteed and what may fail.
These principles matter more for conversational apps because the UI is stateful and fast-moving: if the schema is ambiguous, clients end up guessing how chat state evolves.
A clean top-level design often looks like this:

This works well because:
- Queries support read-heavy chat UIs.
- Mutations map to user actions and backend orchestration.
- Subscriptions handle streaming updates, which is a natural GraphQL fit for conversational apps when you want token deltas or lifecycle events.
A conversation is your durable application object, not Claude’s object. Claude’s Messages API is stateless, so your backend must own conversation persistence and history assembly.

type Conversation implements Node {
  id: ID!
  tenantId: ID!
  title: String
  status: ConversationStatus!
  createdAt: DateTime!
  updatedAt: DateTime!
  latestRun: Run
  messages(first: Int!, after: String): MessageConnection!
  usage(window: UsageWindowInput): UsageSummary!
  metadata: ConversationMetadata
}

is paginated, which GraphQL best practice recommends for lists.
-

supports common UI needs without requiring another query.
-
makes billing/observability first-class for enterprise chat apps.
A message should be a normalized app-level type, even if internally it maps to Claude content blocks.

type Message implements Node {
  id: ID!
  conversationId: ID!
  role: MessageRole!
  content: [MessageContentBlock!]!
  state: MessageState!
  createdAt: DateTime!
  author: MessageAuthor!
  runId: ID
  usage: MessageUsage
}

Because Claude content may include text, images, tool use, and tool results, a union/interface pattern is appropriate here, which is exactly the kind of abstraction GraphQL schema guidance recommends for polymorphic concepts.

union MessageContentBlock =
    TextBlock
  | ImageBlock
  | ToolUseBlock
  | ToolResultBlock
This is better than flattening everything into
, because it preserves future flexibility when content types grow.
A run represents one Claude call or one orchestrated turn.

type Run implements Node {
  id: ID!
  conversationId: ID!
  model: String!
  status: RunStatus!
  stopReason: String
  startedAt: DateTime!
  completedAt: DateTime
  inputMessages: [Message!]!
  outputMessage: Message
  usage: UsageSummary!
  tools: [ToolExecution!]!
  error: RunError
}
This gives clients explicit insight into execution lifecycle, retries, and tool loops without exposing raw provider details.
GraphQL schema design guidance strongly favors using one
object per mutation. That is especially helpful in Claude apps because send-message flows often grow over time.

Why this works:
- It aligns to a user action.
- It leaves room for attachments, idempotency, and future options.
- It returns the exact objects the UI likely needs next.
Use a separate mutation when sending a message and executing a run are different business actions, for example in agentic or approval workflows.

input CreateRunInput {
  conversationId: ID!
  model: String
  systemPromptProfileId: ID
  toolChoice: ToolChoiceInput
  maxTokens: Int
  temperature: Float
}
This lets you separate drafting, moderation, and execution phases instead of forcing everything through a single chat mutation.
Claude supports streaming partial output, and conversational apps often want token-by-token or block-by-block UI updates. In GraphQL, the clean pattern is to expose

union RunEvent =
    RunStartedEvent
  | TextDeltaEvent
  | ToolUseRequestedEvent
  | ToolResultNeededEvent
  | RunCompletedEvent
  | RunFailedEvent

This is better than a single loose JSON blob because:
- Clients can strongly type each event.
- Different UI components can subscribe only to the events they care about.
- The schema becomes self-documenting.
That maps well to token streaming or chunk streaming for chat UIs.
Because this topic sits inside backend integration patterns, multi-tenancy should be represented
, not necessarily exposed everywhere in the public schema. Tenant isolation should usually come from auth context and resolver filters rather than asking clients for a

Good pattern:
- JWT/session resolves tenant in context.
-

returns only if conversation belongs to caller’s tenant.
-

automatically scopes to tenant.
- Admin fields may expose tenant metadata only when policy allows.
for normal users, because that encourages clients to think tenant scope is caller-controlled.
A public schema may still include tenant-aware admin objects for platform operators, but normal conversational schemas should keep tenant context implicit and server-enforced.
Claude’s Messages API supports structured content and tools, so GraphQL should embrace structured content rather than forcing all chat into plain strings.
In practice, you may prefer separate input unions through tagged objects because GraphQL does not support true input unions cleanly. The common workaround is a tagged input pattern:

input MessageInputBlock {
  kind: MessageInputBlockKind!
  text: String
  fileId: ID
  toolUseId: ID
  json: JSON
}
This is not as elegant as output unions, but it remains practical and versionable.
Conversational AI apps need usage visibility. Claude responses expose usage-related fields, and your backend should normalize them into schema types clients can rely on.

type UsageSummary {
  inputTokens: Int!
  outputTokens: Int!
  cacheCreationInputTokens: Int
  cacheReadInputTokens: Int
  estimatedCostUsd: Float
  latencyMs: Int
}
as rollups, and maybe on tenant/admin analytics queries for enterprise dashboards.

This is useful for:
- per-message token indicators,
- billing dashboards,
- cost-aware UX,
- debugging prompt bloat.
GraphQL schema design docs emphasize explicit thinking about nullability and failure modes. For Claude apps, use typed errors rather than “everything nullable and hope for the best.”
You can also return domain payloads with typed result/error unions, especially for actions like
where moderation, quota, or auth failures are common.
Here is a compact but strong example:
This is intentionally app-centric, typed, and extensible, which is exactly what GraphQL design guidance recommends.
so clients can reason about execution lifecycle.
for message history and conversation lists.
for content blocks and stream events.
tenant scoping implicit in auth context
Mirroring Anthropic JSON directly into GraphQL types.
Returning only raw text, losing tool/use structure.
Mixing message persistence and run execution in ways clients cannot observe.
field for all events and content when strong types are possible.

Think of the GraphQL schema as the menu the frontend uses to talk to your AI backend. A bad menu says, “Here is the provider’s raw kitchen protocol, good luck.” A good menu says, “You can create a conversation, send a message, watch the assistant stream, inspect usage, and recover from failures.” That is why good GraphQL schema design for Claude apps focuses on conversations, messages, runs, and events—not just prompts and strings.
Why not just mirror the Claude Messages API in GraphQL?
Because GraphQL schemas should reflect product use cases and frontend workflows, while the Claude API is a lower-level transport contract.
Because a conversation is durable app state, while a run is one execution lifecycle with status, usage, and possible tool interactions.
Why use unions for content and events?
Because Claude interactions are polymorphic—text, tool use, tool results, deltas—and GraphQL unions model that clearly and safely.
Because message history and conversation indexes grow quickly, and GraphQL schema best practices recommend paginated lists by default.
In auth context and resolver enforcement, not as a client-controlled field in everyday chat operations.
